claims.claim_text: The opening breach story identifies a 2021 financial-services CI/CD compromise with Docker-image, bash-uploader, privileged-runner, and two-month exfiltration specifics.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In 2021, a major financial services company suffered a catastrophic software supply chain breach when an advanced persistent threat group compromised their self-hosted CI/CD environment. The attackers exploited a vulnerability in the Docker image creation process, gaining access to a bash uploader script used deep within their automated pipeline. Because the build runners had excessive permissions, operated in privileged mode, and lacked proper namespace isolation, the attackers successfully extracted administrative credentials, deployment tokens, and intellectual property from thousands of customer environments over a period of two months.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Recent software supply-chain incidents have shown that compromising CI/CD tooling can expose build secrets and create downstream customer risk when runner isolation and credential scoping are weak.
claims.rationale: As written this anecdote conflates incident-specific details that are not reliably supportable under the stated named-entity framing.

claims.claim_text: The breach supposedly caused tens of millions of dollars in direct losses.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The financial impact of this breach ran into the tens of millions of dollars in external audit fees, complete infrastructure remediation, and lost enterprise contracts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The downstream costs of a CI/CD compromise can include incident response, remediation, customer communication, and lost business.
claims.rationale: The precise loss framing is not supportable for the incident as written and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: Argo graduated from CNCF on December 6, 2022.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know
claims.rationale: The CNCF announcement directly states Argo graduated on December 6, 2022.

claims.claim_text: Flux graduated from CNCF on November 30, 2022.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know
claims.rationale: The CNCF announcement directly states Flux graduated on November 30, 2022.

claims.claim_text: Tekton became a CNCF incubating project and that status was announced on March 24, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know
claims.rationale: The CNCF post directly announces Tekton's incubation on March 24, 2026.

claims.claim_text: Tekton has a control-plane footprint of roughly 100 MB per controller.
claims.span_hint: section: The Landscape of Self-Hosted CI/CD, platform table
claims.suggested_rewrite: Low relative controller overhead (exact usage depends on workload and configuration)
claims.rationale: The exact memory figure is environment-sensitive and not backed by an authoritative source here.

claims.claim_text: Jenkins has a 1 GB+ baseline control-plane footprint.
claims.span_hint: section: The Landscape of Self-Hosted CI/CD, platform table
claims.rationale: The baseline memory number is deployment-dependent and should not be presented as a fixed fact without a benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: The Woodpecker row assigns an exact very-low control-plane footprint.
claims.span_hint: section: The Landscape of Self-Hosted CI/CD, platform table
claims.anchor_text: | **Woodpecker CI** | Server + RPC Agents | YAML (Drone-like) | Very Low (<50MB) | Lightweight, container-first workflows tightly coupled to Git events. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Woodpecker CI** | Server + RPC Agents | YAML (Drone-like) | Very low relative control-plane overhead | Lightweight, container-first workflows tightly coupled to Git events. |
claims.rationale: The exact memory number is unsourced and overly precise for a configuration-sensitive control plane.

claims.claim_text: The Drone row assigns an exact very-low control-plane footprint and a specific Harness licensing limitation.
claims.span_hint: section: The Landscape of Self-Hosted CI/CD, platform table
claims.anchor_text: | **Drone** | Server + RPC Agents | YAML (Drone format) | Very Low (<50MB) | Similar to Woodpecker, but subject to Harness enterprise licensing limits. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Drone** | Server + RPC Agents | YAML (Drone format) | Very low relative control-plane overhead | Similar agent-based workflows for teams already standardized on Drone. |
claims.rationale: This combines an unsourced benchmark with a licensing assertion that needs a primary vendor source.

claims.claim_text: Gitea Actions is GitHub-Actions-compatible, uses act_runner, and runner-to-server communication uses gRPC over HTTP.
claims.span_hint: section: Gitea Actions: The Compatibility Layer
claims.rationale: The exact compatibility and protocol details are documented in Gitea's official docs, which are off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module claims act_runner creates Kubernetes pods via Docker-API translation or directly via the Kubernetes API.
claims.span_hint: section: Gitea Actions: The Compatibility Layer, Cons bullet
claims.anchor_text: The Kubernetes execution environment for `act_runner` creates pods dynamically via the Docker API translation or directly via the Kubernetes API.
claims.suggested_rewrite: GitHub-Actions-style workflows can become harder to debug when complex multi-container networking patterns are adapted to self-hosted runners.
claims.rationale: The cited official docs describe Docker and host runner modes, not this specific Kubernetes-API translation claim.

claims.claim_text: GitLab Runner is a single binary with no language-specific requirements and supports the listed operating systems and CPU architectures.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: GitLab Runner on Kubernetes, paragraphs 1-2
claims.rationale: These exact support statements come from GitLab's official docs, which are off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The GitLab Kubernetes executor creates one pod per CI job and needs create/list/watch and related Kubernetes permissions.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: GitLab Runner on Kubernetes, paragraphs 3-4
claims.rationale: The per-job pod model and RBAC requirements are documented in GitLab's official Kubernetes executor docs, which are off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: GitLab recommends keeping GitLab Runner major.minor in sync with GitLab major.minor.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: GitLab Runner on Kubernetes, final sentence
claims.rationale: The compatibility guidance is an official GitLab product statement from an off-allowlist host.

claims.claim_text: The Jenkins Kubernetes plugin provisions a pod per agent, uses an inbound `jnlp` agent by default, and does not require the controller to run inside Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: Jenkins on Kubernetes, paragraphs 1-3
claims.rationale: These exact plugin behaviors are documented on Jenkins plugin docs, which are off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Jenkins Configuration as Code should define controller state instead of relying on UI-based configuration.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: Jenkins on Kubernetes, Operational Characteristics
claims.rationale: The primary source for this recommendation is Jenkins documentation on an off-allowlist host.

claims.claim_text: Jenkins controller garbage-collection pauses can cause missed webhooks or dropped agent connections.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: Jenkins on Kubernetes, Cons bullet
claims.anchor_text: Garbage collection pauses on the master can cause missed webhooks or dropped agent connections.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Long JVM pauses or an undersized Jenkins controller can destabilize webhook handling and agent connectivity under load.
claims.rationale: The exact causal wording is not backed here and should be generalized to an operational risk statement.

claims.claim_text: Tekton runs as a Kubernetes extension built from Custom Resources, and its `TaskRun`/`PipelineRun` model is Kubernetes-native with per-task pod execution.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: Tekton Pipelines (CRD Native), paragraphs 1-2
claims.rationale: The section pool source directly covers Tekton's CRD-based architecture and per-task pod execution.

claims.claim_text: Tekton pipeline execution state is stored in Kubernetes API objects, so retaining huge numbers of completed runs consumes cluster state storage.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: Tekton Pipelines (CRD Native), paragraph 1; stop-and-think prompt
claims.rationale: Tekton resources are Custom Resources and Kubernetes documents that API resource state is persisted in etcd.

claims.claim_text: Tekton Workspaces can use PVCs, and a shared `ReadWriteOnce` workspace constrains concurrent tasks because the volume can only be mounted on one node at a time.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: Tekton Pipelines (CRD Native), Cons and Production Gotcha bullets
claims.rationale: The pool covers Workspaces generally, and Tekton's Workspace docs directly describe PVC-backed workspaces and `ReadWriteOnce` same-node constraints.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD is a declarative GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes that uses Git as the desired-state source of truth and reconciles cluster state.
claims.span_hint: section: Deep Dive: GitOps with Argo CD and Flux
claims.rationale: Argo CD's official docs directly describe it as a declarative GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: Buildah requires specific seccomp profiles or `securityContext.privileged: true` when host user namespaces are unavailable.
claims.span_hint: section: Core CI/CD Challenges on Bare Metal, Unprivileged Container Builds
claims.anchor_text: Requires specific seccomp profiles or `securityContext.privileged: true` if unprivileged user namespaces are not enabled on the host kernel.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Containerized Buildah often needs host and security-context tuning to work cleanly in Kubernetes, especially when rootless user-namespace support is unavailable.
claims.rationale: The precise privileged-mode rule is too environment-specific to assert here without a narrow primary source.

claims.claim_text: Kaniko executes Dockerfile steps in userspace without a Docker daemon, and Docker registry auth can be provided via `/kaniko/.docker` configuration.
claims.span_hint: section: Core CI/CD Challenges on Bare Metal, Unprivileged Container Builds; Kaniko Implementation Detail
claims.rationale: The Kaniko project README directly documents daemonless userspace execution and `/kaniko/.docker` auth configuration.

claims.claim_text: External Secrets Operator can synchronize secrets into the CI namespace.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Injection
claims.rationale: The exact ESO capability is documented on the project's official site, which is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Vault Agent sidecars can inject secrets into pods via a mutating webhook and shared volume.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Injection
claims.rationale: HashiCorp's Vault Injector docs directly describe sidecar-based pod mutation and shared-volume secret rendering.

claims.claim_text: Building `arm64` images on `amd64` nodes with QEMU is over 10x slower for compiled languages.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Architecture Builds; Common Mistakes table
claims.anchor_text: Building `arm64` images on `amd64` nodes using QEMU emulation is prohibitively slow for compiled languages (Rust, Go, C++).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Building `arm64` images on `amd64` nodes via emulation can be much slower than routing work to native `arm64` hardware.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the strength of the performance claim needs a benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: Mounting `/var/run/docker.sock` into a CI pod leads to instant root privilege escalation.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, first row
claims.anchor_text: | Mounting `/var/run/docker.sock` | Exposes the host kernel directly to the CI pod, leading to instant root privilege escalation. | Use rootless Buildah or Kaniko. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Mounting `/var/run/docker.sock` | Gives the CI pod broad control over the host container runtime and breaks normal isolation boundaries. | Use rootless Buildah or Kaniko. |
claims.rationale: The exact escalation wording is overstated as written and should be reduced to the defensible isolation risk.

claims.claim_text: Bare-metal storage classes often add 10-30 seconds of detach/attach latency between Tekton tasks that share a PVC.
claims.span_hint: section: Practitioner Gotchas, item 1
claims.anchor_text: When running pipelines that pass state between tasks via PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs), bare-metal storage classes (like Rook-Ceph block or Longhorn) often take 10-30 seconds to detach a volume from the Node running Task A and attach it to the Node running Task B.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When pipelines pass state between tasks via a shared PVC, volume detach/attach delays can add noticeable overhead if successive tasks land on different nodes.
claims.rationale: The exact latency band is environment-specific and should not be stated as a general fact without measurement data.

claims.claim_text: Cleanup failures can leave Tekton or Jenkins pods lingering permanently and exhausting inode or IP limits.
claims.span_hint: section: Practitioner Gotchas, item 2
claims.anchor_text: Tekton and Jenkins Kubernetes agents create Pods that are supposed to be cleaned up after execution. Network partitions or OOM kills of the controller can leave `Completed` or `Error` pods lingering permanently, eventually exhausting node inode or IP limits.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Tekton and Jenkins Kubernetes agents can leave stale pods behind when cleanup paths fail, so operators should monitor CI namespaces and prune abandoned resources.
claims.rationale: The general cleanup risk is useful, but the specific failure chain and permanence claim are not sourced here.

claims.claim_text: Kaniko loads full filesystem state into memory, large images will OOMKill the pod, and `--snapshot-mode=redo` lowers memory usage.
claims.span_hint: section: Practitioner Gotchas, item 3
claims.anchor_text: Kaniko loads the entire filesystem state into memory during layer calculation. Building large images (e.g., machine learning containers with gigabytes of dependencies) will OOMKill the Kaniko pod if resource limits are set too tightly. *Fix:* Remove memory limits entirely for Kaniko pods, or implement the `--snapshot-mode=redo` flag which trades CPU time for lower memory consumption during file system delta calculation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large image builds can exhaust Kaniko pod memory, so resource sizing and Kaniko snapshot and caching settings should be tested against the real build context.
claims.rationale: The exact memory mechanism and mitigation claims are stronger than the upstream docs directly support.

claims.claim_text: Lightweight CI servers default to SQLite and are claimed to corrupt on bad storage or non-graceful termination, losing all history and registration state.
claims.span_hint: section: Practitioner Gotchas, item 4
claims.anchor_text: By default, lightweight CI servers often default to local SQLite databases. If scheduled on storage prone to high IO latency or non-graceful pod terminations, the database corrupts, losing all pipeline history and agent registration state. *Fix:* Always configure external Postgres or MySQL databases for the CI control plane on production bare-metal clusters.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Some lightweight CI servers default to embedded SQLite databases, which is convenient for evaluation but a poor fit for a production CI control plane; use an external database for serious deployments.
claims.rationale: The default-database point is real for some products, but the corruption and total-data-loss claim is too strong as written.

claims.claim_text: The lab requires Kubernetes v1.32+.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-on Lab, Prerequisites
claims.suggested_rewrite: A running, recent Kubernetes cluster compatible with the installed Tekton release.
claims.rationale: A hard minimum version needs a compatibility source and should not be left as an unexplained exact cutoff.

further_reading.title: Tekton Pipelines
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for Tekton CRDs, TaskRuns, PipelineRuns, Workspaces, and execution semantics.

further_reading.title: Kaniko README
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents daemonless image building, registry authentication options, and core Kaniko runtime behavior.

further_reading.title: OCI Distribution Spec
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines the standard registry API underlying self-hosted OCI image distribution workflows.

further_reading.title: Assigning Pods to Nodes
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the node labels and scheduling primitives used to route CI jobs to architecture-specific hardware.

further_reading.title: Vault Agent Injector
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for runtime secret injection via sidecar and mutating-webhook patterns on Kubernetes.

notes: Major platform-specific sections rely on official docs outside the current allowlist: docs.github.com, docs.gitea.com, docs.gitlab.com, jenkins.io/plugins.jenkins.io, external-secrets.io, woodpecker-ci.org, and docs.drone.io. Several table values and practitioner-gotcha numbers are illustrative or environment-dependent and should be generalized unless a benchmark source is added.
